title: Chapter 15: The Liberation
content: The interviews took three days.

ADA Maria Okonkwo set up a temporary office in the cafeteria — the same cafeteria where Chloe had counted seventeen patients on her first day, where she'd met Layla and Sophia and Maya at Table Four. The orderlies were gone, replaced by county deputies. The cameras were still blinking their red heartbeats, but now the footage was being pulled by the DA's office as evidence.

Sterling's lawyer — the fourth one, after the first three quit — had tried to block the interviews. Patient confidentiality, he'd argued. HIPAA. The therapeutic relationship. Maria had forwarded his motion to a judge who'd denied it in forty-five minutes, citing "extraordinary circumstances and credible evidence of institutional abuse."
The phrase *credible evidence* was, Maria knew, a gift from a judge who wanted to give her everything she needed.
Chloe was interviewed first. She was still groggy — the yellow pill took forty-eight hours to fully clear the system — but her mind was coming back. The sharp edges returning. The fog lifting.
"Start from the beginning," Maria said. "Day one."
Chloe started from the handcuffs.
The other patients came next.

Sophia told Maria about the forty-seven-year-old hedge fund manager, the three family dinners where her father had presented the marriage as "an opportunity," the eight months of "treatment" that had consisted entirely of being told that her refusal was a symptom of her illness.

Maya told Maria about the state championships, the coaches who'd called her "intimidating," the mother who'd signed commitment papers because winning shot put wasn't "ladylike" and nothing Maya did would ever be ladylike enough.

Layla told Maria about the surveillance startup, the father who'd called her a "waste of talent" when she refused to build it, the lithium prescription she'd been on for fourteen months for a condition she'd never been diagnosed with.

Rachel told Maria everything — the Lancet paper, the commitment three months later, the three years of memorizing files while pretending to be compliant. She recited patient names and intake reasons from memory, forty cases, every detail intact.

By the end of the third day, Maria had enough evidence to charge Sterling with forty-seven counts of fraudulent imprisonment, seventeen counts of assault with a deadly weapon — the olanzapine — and enough billing fraud to keep a forensic accountant busy for a year.
Sterling was arrested in his office at 4:00 PM on day three.

The deputies walked him out through the lobby — the same lobby Chloe had entered fourteen days ago, the same lavender-scented air, the same framed quote about surrender still hanging on the wall. He was in handcuffs. Real ones. Police-grade.
Chloe watched from the hallway, flanked by Layla and Sophia and Maya and Vasquez and Rachel. Six women who had, in fourteen days, dismantled an institution that had been operating for twelve years.

Sterling saw her. His eyes met hers across the lobby — the same lobby where she'd smiled at Holloway on day one, where Anna Delgado had touched the rotated plant, where the camera had blinked its red heartbeat through every moment of Chloe's war.

"This isn't over," Sterling said. His voice was calm — the same calm he'd used in his office, the same calm he'd used at the patient assembly. The calm of a man who still believed his money and his lawyers and his network of wealthy families would protect him.
"It is for you," Chloe said.

Sterling's jaw tightened. "You think you've won. You've made noise. You've gotten attention. But the families who sent their daughters here? They're still out there. They're still rich. They're still powerful. And they're not going to let a TikToker and a hacker and a shot-putter destroy what they've built."
Chloe smiled. The weaponized one.
"You're right," she said. "They're still out there. But we're out here now. And once you've been locked up and gotten out, you're not afraid of being locked up anymore. You're just... free."
